attributes.stem:
An 89-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of colicky abdominal pain and absolute constipation. He has a background of widespread metastatic prostate cancer, which has progressed despite androgen deprivation therapy. He also has a mechanical mitral valve replacement and is taking warfarin. He is not on any other medications.

He appears frail and unwell. Observations are: pulse 95/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO₂ 96% on room air, temperature 37.2°C. Abdominal imaging confirms large and small bowel obstruction. A firm, fixed mass is palpated on digital rectal examination. He undergoes a defunctioning ileostomy. Although he recovers from surgery, his overall condition remains poor.

The patient is alert and cooperative, but speaks limited English. His son, who speaks fluent English, insists that his father is stating he wants "everything done to stay alive" and asks the team not to upset him with talk of death or poor prognosis. The medical team believes that intensive care support is not appropriate given the patient's prognosis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Respect the son's request to avoid distressing the patient
Ask the son to translate carefully, given his insight into his father's preferences
Seek input from an ethics committee about overriding family wishes
Ask the views of other family members
Get an interpreter involved to discuss the situation directly with the patient
attributes.explanation:
Discussions regarding goals of care and end of life decisions are commonplace in the ICU. This case highlights the importance of appropriate communication in end-of-life care discussions. The patient has decision-making capacity but limited English proficiency, creating a communication barrier that must be appropriately addressed.

In the first instance, if the patent is competent to make decisions for themselves, these discussions should take place directly with the patient. The standard of care if the patient does not speak English is to use a professional interpreter from outside the family. Professional interpreters are trained to provide accurate, unbiased translation and are essential for informed consent and shared decision-making. This ensures the patient can directly participate in discussions about his care and make informed decisions about his treatment.

Asking the son to translate carefully is inappropriate as family members should not be used as interpreters, particularly for serious medical discussions. They may have their own emotional involvement, conflicting views, or may not accurately translate medical information. However, with the patient's consent, it is often beneficial, for both the patient and treating team, to include family members in these discussions.

Similarly respecting the son's request to avoid distressing the patient would be inappropriate as the patient has capacity to make his own decisions. The son's wishes may not reflect the patient's own preferences regarding end-of-life care.

Seeking input from an ethics committee about overriding family wishes is premature at this stage. Before escalating to an ethics committee, direct communication with the patient through a professional interpreter should be attempted.

Asking the views of other family members would be inappropriate as the patient has capacity to make his own decisions. While family input can be valuable, the patient's autonomy must be respected.

In cases where the patient does not have the capacity to make decisions about their treatment or welfare an informed decision must still be made, but by a substitute decision maker. Such a substitute decision maker may have been previously appointed by the patient in an Advanced Care Directive. Commonly, if an Advanced Care Directive is not in place, then a responsible person (spouse, next of kin, relative or close friend) may be suitable to make decisions for the patient so long as they had a close and enduring relationship with the patient and are willing to make decisions that they believe would have aligned with the patient's wishes.

If no one can be found that fulfils the above roles, or if there is dispute amongst eligible decision makers, an application for a tribunal hearing is made for the appointment of a guardian who can then make those decisions.

Healthcare providers have an ethical and legal obligation to ensure effective communication with patients who have limited English proficiency. This includes using professional interpreters for important medical discussions, particularly those involving end-of-life care and treatment decisions.
learningPoints: Professional interpreters must be used for discussions about goals of care with patients who have limited English proficiency.
Family members should not be used as interpreters due to potential conflicts of interest and risk of inaccurate translation.
If a patient has decision-making capacity, discussions should occur directly with them regardless of language barriers.
references: Caulley L, Gillick M, Lehmann L. Substitute Decision Making in End-of-Life Care. New England Journal of Medicine. 2018;378(24):2339-2341. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29897848/

Barwise A, Nyquist C, Espinoza Suarez N, Jaramillo C, Thorsteinsdottir B, Gajic O et al. End-of-Life Decision-Making for ICU Patients With Limited English Proficiency. Critical Care Medicine. 2019;47(10):1380-1387. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31389838/

Lundin C, Hadziabdic E, Hjelm K. Language interpretation conditions and boundaries in multilingual and multicultural emergency healthcare. BMC International Health and Human Rights. 2018;18(1). Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29866163/
